title: HESTA backs $70m VC fund for med tech stars
full_text:
Superannuation giant HESTA is the major investor in a new venture capital firm that has banked close to $60 million for its debut fund and will target early stage Australian health and medical technology start-ups.
Synthesis Capital is targeting a total of $70 million for its first fund and has taken two years to reach this stage, after also raising funds from family offices, global health industry executives and the Menzies Leadership Foundation.
The firm is Melbourne-based and led by Vishaal Kishore and Buzz Palmer - who previously co-founded the MedTech Actuator, a government-funded accelerator program to help founders commercialise their ideas - alongside former Salesforce executive Robert Wickham and Tom Bentley, the deputy chief of staff to former prime minister Julia Gillard.
Makenzie Thomas, Rebecca Davies, Yong Yi Xuan and Lizzie Laurence have also joined the Synthesis Capital team.
Superannuation funds such as HESTA, Hostplus and AustralianSuper are the biggest backers of Australia's tech start-up scene. However, it is uncommon for a first-time fund manager such as Synthesis Capital to attract one of them before proving itself.
HESTA's chief investment officer, Sonya Sawtell-Rickson, said the VC team's proven ability to attract government funding for its medtech accelerator program, and their established relationships across Australian medical research institutions, universities and hospitals had helped it through the super fund's due diligence process.
"We believe Synthesis Capital brings the specialist focus and deep market relationships that are crucial for more investment in early-stage medtech and deep health technology," she said.
HESTA's members are mostly women working in the health, nursing and care sector. The superannuation giant is also a major backer of Blackbird Ventures and life sciences investor Brandon Capital Partners.
The capital allocated to Synthesis Capital must be invested in Australian scientists and entrepreneurs pioneering new medical and health technologies.
Kishore, who was previously deputy secretary of Victoria's Department of Health and first chief strategy officer of start-up agency LaunchVic, said the fund would help connect Australian scientists with global investors.
"We're interested in technologies that have a regulated pathway and some kind of IP packet that is being commercialised," Kishore said.
"In health, Australian breakthrough companies are rarely limited by science. They're much more often limited by access to capital, access to capability, and access to local and international networks. That's what we bring."
The fund will operate as an early stage limited venture capital partnership, an investment vehicle that provides capital gains tax concessions.
Despite the sector's opposition to the recent federal budget changes, Kishore said VC funds may become more attractive than other investment options, but he added that the measures would be a "disaster" for start-up founders.
Kishore said he had noticed a "chilling effect" among investors since the Albanese government announced plans to remove the 50 per cent discount on capital gains tax and replace it with an inflation-adjusted model.
"Even though there are capital gains tax benefits with an ESVCLP [early stage venture capital limited partnership], investors are just uncertain because they don't know what their real investment profile is right now. Are they going to have to pay twice as much tax on their crypto investments?" he said.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Tess Bennett
heading: Superannuation and Investments
